Patient Summary: This 59-year-old male with a history of EtOH-induced cirrhosis and Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) was admitted for a scheduled deceased donor liver transplant (DDLT). On admission, he was hemodynamically stable with normal renal function (Cr 0.7) and mild anemia (Hgb 11.5). 
 Surgical Course: The patient underwent a DDLT using the piggyback technique. His post-operative course was complicated, requiring several invasive interventions: 1) Re-exploration and right hemidiaphragm plication on POD 1 to relieve IVC obstruction; 2) A pericardial window with drain placement for a loculated effusion causing tamponade signs; 3) ERCP with biliary stent placement to address an anastomotic bile leak; and 4) CT-guided pigtail catheter placement for a mid-abdominal bilious fluid collection.
 Clinical Management and Complications: 
 - Cardiovascular: Managed Atrial Fibrillation with RVR (Digoxin/Propranolol) and a pericardial effusion.
 - Pulmonary: Successfully treated brief acute respiratory failure with diuresis.
 - Gastrointestinal: Addressed a biliary leak/collection and managed immunosuppression-related diarrhea by switching from Cellcept to Myfortic.
 - Neurological: Treated post-operative delirium with Seroquel, which was successfully weaned.
 - Infectious Disease: Managed low-grade fevers with a course of Ciprofloxacin; all cultures remained negative.
 Laboratory and Medication Status: At discharge, liver function had significantly improved (TotBili 0.5, AST 33), while creatinine stabilized at 1.1. Tacrolimus troughs were maintained between 8.7-9.5. The patient was discharged on a stable immunosuppression regimen consisting of Tacrolimus, Myfortic, and a Prednisone taper, along with standard post-transplant prophylaxis (Valganciclovir, Fluconazole, and Bactrim). He remains stable with an abdominal pigtail drain and a biliary stent in place for outpatient follow-up.

On admission, he was hemodynamically stable with normal renal function (Cr 0.7) and mild anemia (Hgb 11.5). 

Surgical Course: The patient underwent a DDLT using the piggyback technique. His post-operative course was complicated, requiring several invasive interventions: 1) Re-exploration and right hemidiaphragm plication on POD 1 to relieve IVC obstruction; 2) A pericardial window with drain placement for a loculated effusion causing tamponade signs; 3) ERCP with biliary stent placement to address an anastomotic bile leak; and 4) CT-guided pigtail catheter placement for a mid-abdominal bilious fluid collection.

Clinical Management and Complications: 
- Cardiovascular: Managed Atrial Fibrillation with RVR (Digoxin/Propranolol) and a pericardial effusion.
- Pulmonary: Successfully treated brief acute respiratory failure with diuresis.
- Gastrointestinal: Addressed a biliary leak/collection and managed immunosuppression-related diarrhea by switching from Cellcept to Myfortic.
- Neurological: Treated post-operative delirium with Seroquel, which was successfully weaned.
- Infectious Disease: Managed low-grade fevers with a course of Ciprofloxacin; all cultures remained negative.

Laboratory and Medication Status: At discharge, liver function had significantly improved (TotBili 0.5, AST 33), while creatinine stabilized at 1.1. Tacrolimus troughs were maintained between 8.7-9.5. The patient was discharged on a stable immunosuppression regimen consisting of Tacrolimus, Myfortic, and a Prednisone taper, along with standard post-transplant prophylaxis (Valganciclovir, Fluconazole, and Bactrim). He remains stable with an abdominal pigtail drain and a biliary stent in place for outpatient follow-up.

Comprehensive Discharge Summary: This 59-year-old male with ETOH cirrhosis and HCC (s/p TIPS, RFA x2) was admitted for a deceased donor liver transplant (DDLT). 
 The patient underwent DDLT (piggyback technique, PV-PV, CHA-CHA, BD-BD anastomosis) with an internal biliary stent. 
 On POD 1, he required surgical re-exploration and right hemidiaphragm plication to resolve suspected IVC obstruction. 
 His postoperative course was significant for multi-system complications. 
 Cardiovascular: Developed atrial fibrillation/atrial flutter with RVR, managed with Digoxin and Propranolol. Echocardiography identified a large loculated pericardial effusion with tamponade signs, necessitating a pericardial window with 200cc fluid drainage (cultures negative). 
 Pulmonary: Experienced acute respiratory failure post-transplant; following diuresis, he was successfully extubated on POD 5. 
 Biliary/GI: A bile leak at the anastomosis was confirmed via ERCP, treated with a 7 Fr biliary stent and sphincterotomy. A mid-abdominal fluid collection (bilirubin 1.4) was drained via CT-guided pigtail catheter, aspirating 120cc of bilious fluid (cultures negative). 
 Neurological: Postoperative delirium/agitation was managed with a Seroquel taper. 
 Significant Lab Trends (Admission vs. Discharge): WBC 4.7 to 5.3 K/uL; Hgb 11.5 to 10.0 g/dL; Cr 0.7 to 1.1 mg/dL; K 4.0 to 4.4 mEq/L; ALT 27 to 69 U/L; AST 38 to 33 U/L; AlkPhos 111 to 74 U/L; and TotBili 1.5 to 0.5 mg/dL. 
 Tacrolimus (FK) trough levels ranged from 8.7 to 13.0 ng/mL, with a discharge level of 8.7 ng/mL. 
 The patient was discharged in stable, ambulatory condition with an abdominal pigtail drain. 
 Medication regimen at discharge includes Tacrolimus, Myfortic (transitioned from Cellcept due to diarrhea), Prednisone, Digoxin, Ciprofloxacin, Valganciclovir, and Sulfameth/Trimethoprim (Bactrim).
